name: Jump Buffering
description: A short input buffer that stores a jump press just before jumping becomes legal.
design_notes: Jump buffering makes landings, wall contacts, and platform arrivals feel responsive. It should be tied to specific jump eligibility events so a stale input does not trigger long after the player intent has passed.
design_purpose: Accept intended jump inputs pressed a little early.
Make landing jumps and wall jumps feel responsive.
Reduce timing frustration without changing level geometry.
player_fantasy: The player feels the game understood the jump they meant to perform.

parameters.name: jump_buffer_ms
parameters.description: How long a queued jump input remains valid.

parameters.name: buffer_accepts_hold
parameters.description: Whether holding the button can create a buffered jump.

parameters.name: buffer_priority_order
parameters.description: Priority used when multiple jump types become legal together.

edge_cases: Buffered jump during hit stun, ladder attach, ledge climb, or other animation locks should usually expire before control returns.
Cutscenes, dialogue choices, pause menus, and UI focus changes must clear or suspend the buffer so a stale jump does not fire after control resumes.
Landing, coyote time, wall contact, and double-jump eligibility can become true in the same simulation tick and need a deterministic priority order.
A buffered jump on a drop-through platform can conflict with downward input.
Assist settings that increase jump_buffer_ms should not let a press survive respawn, checkpoint restore, room transition, or save/load.
Multiple queued actions need a priority rule so jump does not steal dash, attack, or interact inputs.
common_bugs: The buffer never clears after an invalid jump and fires seconds later.
Hold-to-jump creates repeated buffered jumps every frame.
The buffer consumes input before variable jump height can read button release.
A buffered jump fires after a cutscene or menu closes because focus-loss events did not clear input history.
Ground detection becomes true one physics step late, so a landing buffer spends a double jump instead of a ground jump.
Coyote time and jump buffering both consume the same input and produce two jump impulses from one button press.
balancing_notes: A small buffer improves feel without changing challenge; a large buffer can make rhythmic timing automatic.
Buffering should be tested with coyote time because the pair changes perceived jump leniency.
accessibility_notes: Expose stronger buffering in assist settings for games with frequent precision jumps.

implementation_notes.unity: Record jump-press timestamps from the Input System, then consume the newest buffered press after ground, wall, ledge, and animation-lock state are resolved in the fixed movement update. Clear buffered input on UI focus, cutscene control, respawn, and scene load.
implementation_notes.godot: Capture Input.is_action_just_pressed for jump into a short-lived buffer and resolve it after floor and wall checks so buffered jumps use the correct movement state. Reset the buffer when control_mode, pause_state, or cutscene ownership changes.
implementation_notes.unreal: Queue jump input in the character or ability component and consume it from CharacterMovement state after landing, wall contact, or custom movement eligibility is confirmed. Include the consumed input sequence in prediction so rollback does not replay the same jump twice.
implementation_notes.web: Store jump_pressed_ms and input_sequence_id in the input state, expire it after jump_buffer_ms, and let the simulation consume it once a legal jump transition is available. Clear it when focus changes or checkpoint restore rewinds movement state.

relationships.reason: Jump buffering complements coyote time by accepting input just before landing or becoming jump-eligible.
Buffered jump starts must feed the same press and release timestamps used by variable jump height.
Buffered jumps need shared jump-consumption priority so landing, air-jump, and wall-jump states do not spend the wrong jump.
Hold, toggle, and assisted input settings can change how long jump intent should remain buffered.
Buffered jumps and dash inputs can conflict when both consume the same button timing or cancel windows.

example_games.title: Celeste
example_games.note: Uses input leniency to support precise platforming.
